scenarios.task: I review my team's numbers in depth at the end of each quarter to see who hit target and who didn't. During the quarter I mostly let reps run their own process and only step in if someone asks for help.
criteria: Flags reviewing performance only at quarter-end as the managing-only-the-lagging-number failure mode
States that pipeline coverage is a leading indicator that would have shown a likely miss months earlier, before it's too late to fix within the quarter
Recommends reviewing pipeline coverage against quota on a fixed, more frequent cadence, not waiting for the lagging revenue number
Notes that waiting for reps to self-identify a problem misses the point — the manager's job includes catching thin coverage proactively

scenarios.task: In our last forecast call, a rep flagged that a big deal she had listed as 'commit' was actually now at risk since a key stakeholder went quiet. I was frustrated in the meeting since that deal was central to our number, and I let her know the team needs to be more careful about what gets called commit in the first place.
criteria: Flags reacting negatively to the honest at-risk flag as the punishing-honest-forecast-reporting failure mode
States that this response trains the team to only report good news, destroying the forecast's usefulness as a real signal going forward
Recommends responding to the flag with problem-solving (what can be done about the stalled stakeholder) rather than frustration about the miscategorization
Reframes psychological safety in forecast conversations as a functional requirement, not a soft nicety, given what's at stake if reps stop reporting honestly

scenarios.task: I split our team's total quota evenly across all six reps, giving everyone the exact same number — that feels like the fairest way to set targets since no one can complain about being treated differently.
criteria: Flags an even split across reps as potentially unfair despite being numerically equal, naming the uneven-quota-treated-as-fair failure mode
States that an even split across territories of different actual potential sets some reps up to fail and others to coast
Recommends grounding quota/territory design in actual bottom-up market/account potential per territory, reconciled with the top-down number
Distinguishes equal treatment (same number) from fair treatment (a number that reflects actual territory potential)

scenarios.task: One of my reps has a huge deal that's stuck, so I've started jumping on the calls myself and driving the negotiation directly since I have more experience closing deals like this and don't want to risk losing it.
criteria: Flags personally taking over the deal as the selling-instead-of-managing failure mode
States that this caps the manager's impact at their own personal capacity instead of the whole team's, and stunts the rep's development
Recommends coaching the rep through the deal (specific next steps, stakeholder strategy) rather than taking it over directly
Distinguishes appropriate manager involvement (strategic coaching, joining a call to support) from replacing the rep as the deal owner
